meta.title: Screw U, ATS
meta.description: MCP server that screens junior developer candidates by analyzing their GitHub profile instead of their CV.

tools.name: score_candidate
tools.summary: Score a GitHub user against a role definition — returns fit_score, recommendation, per-signal breakdown, matched/missing concepts, and a trajectory summary.
tools.description: Fetches the candidate's public GitHub repos, runs trajectory, concept-match, and complexity scoring against the specified role definition, and returns a structured hiring signal. fit_score (0–100) is the weighted combination of all three signals (trajectory 45%, concept match 35%, complexity 20%). A fit_score of 50 or higher yields a recommendation of Interview.

inputs.name: github_username
inputs.description: GitHub username of the candidate to evaluate

inputs.name: role
inputs.description: Role to evaluate against. Accepted values: junior-frontend, junior-fullstack, junior-csharp

inputs.name: include_lighthouse
inputs.description: Run Lighthouse audits on live project URLs found in the candidate's repos. Default: false. Results appear in enrichment.lighthouse and have no effect on fit_score.

returns.description: Structured hiring signal with fit score, recommendation, per-signal breakdown, matched and missing concepts, and a plain-English trajectory summary. When include_lighthouse is true, an enrichment.lighthouse block is added with PageSpeed scores and WCAG violations per live URL.

shape.enrichment: (optional) { lighthouse: { live_projects_found: number, audits: [{ url: string, scores: { performance: number, accessibility: number, best_practices: number, seo: number }, wcag_violations: string[] }] } }

output.trajectory_summary: Positive learning trajectory — recent projects are noticeably more complex than earlier ones.

tools.name: candidate_profile
tools.summary: Return a role-agnostic GitHub profile for a candidate — repo count, active dates, top languages, skill map, average complexity, trajectory summary, top repos, and suggested roles.
tools.description: Fetches a candidate's public GitHub repos and returns a structured overview without requiring a role to be specified. Useful for a quick read on a candidate before deciding which role to score them against. Returns skill_map (five axes: frontend, backend, devops, testing, architecture), trajectory_summary, top_repos by complexity, top_languages, and suggested_roles derived from the skill_map and average complexity.

inputs.name: graduation_date
inputs.description: ISO date (YYYY-MM-DD) when the candidate graduated. Repos before this date are weighted lower in trajectory scoring.

returns.description: Role-agnostic candidate profile with skill map, trajectory summary, top repos, and suggested roles to score against.

output.trajectory_summary: Positive learning trajectory — recent projects are noticeably more complex than earlier ones.

tools.name: search_knowledge
tools.summary: Search the knowledge base for role definitions and documentation.
tools.description: Full-text searches all markdown files in the knowledge/ directory, including role definitions under knowledge/roles/. Requires DATABASE_URL to be set — without it, returns an error explaining the requirement.

returns.description: Ranked list of matching excerpts with source file and section heading.

content.body: Screw U, ATS is an MCP server that screens junior developer candidates by analyzing their public GitHub profile instead of their CV. ATS systems filter on keywords; junior devs and career-changers often have no work history but strong GitHub activity. This server surfaces that signal.

Give it a GitHub username and a role (e.g. junior-frontend), and it returns a scored hiring signal: a fit_score (0–100), a recommendation (Interview / Pass), a per-signal breakdown, matched and missing concepts, and a plain-English trajectory summary describing how the candidate's complexity has grown over time.

items.label: score_candidate
items.summary: Score a GitHub user against a role definition — returns fit_score, recommendation, per-signal breakdown, matched/missing concepts, and trajectory summary

items.label: search_knowledge
items.summary: Full-text search across role definitions in knowledge/roles/ (requires DATABASE_URL)

content.body: The server runs on stdio by default. Set PORT to also expose an HTTP+SSE endpoint. All tools go through a ToolRuntime that centralises error handling, write-gating, and response formatting. Optional PostgreSQL and Redis connections enable audit logging and caching.

items: GitHub client — fetches public profile and repos, detects CI and test presence per repo
Complexity signals — scores each repo on tests, CI, dependency maturity, README quality, commit span
Concept matcher — parses role definition markdown and matches against repo languages, READMEs, and deps
Trajectory algorithm — buckets repos by age, calculates complexity delta, weights recent repos 3x

Lighthouse enrichment — opt-in: extracts live project URLs from READMEs and package.json, audits them via PageSpeed Insights API, returns per-URL Lighthouse scores and WCAG violations (no effect on fit_score)
Role definitions — plain markdown in knowledge/roles/, community-contributable
stdio transport — default, works with Claude Desktop, Cursor, and any MCP client
HTTP+SSE transport — optional, enabled by setting PORT
PostgreSQL — optional, enables audit log and knowledge search
Redis — optional, enables GitHub response caching
Connect your MCP client to the server via stdio

content.label: Minimum .env
content.body: GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_your_token_here   # required — read:user and public_repo scopes
The server will refuse to start if GITHUB_TOKEN is missing. Create a classic token at github.com/settings/tokens with read:user and public_repo scopes. No write permissions needed.
Set PORT to run an HTTP+SSE server alongside stdio. Useful for web-based MCP clients or multiple concurrent sessions.

content.label: .env (HTTP mode)
content.body:
GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_your_token_here
PORT=3000
MCP_SECRET=your-secret   # optional: require x-mcp-secret header

items: POST /mcp — MCP protocol endpoint (requires x-mcp-secret header if MCP_SECRET is set)
GET /audit — audit log dashboard (requires Authorization: Bearer <MCP_SECRET>)

content.body: All configuration is via environment variables. Copy .env.example to .env to get started. GITHUB_TOKEN is the only required variable.

vars.name: GITHUB_TOKEN
vars.description: GitHub personal access token — required to fetch candidate profiles and repos. Needs read:user and public_repo scopes.

vars.name: ALLOW_WRITES
vars.description: Set to true to enable write tools (tools that modify data)

vars.name: PORT
vars.description: Enables HTTP+SSE transport on this port alongside stdio

vars.name: DB_TYPE
vars.description: Storage adapter. Set to postgres to enable audit log and knowledge search.

vars.name: DATABASE_URL
vars.description: PostgreSQL connection string — used when DB_TYPE=postgres

vars.name: REDIS_URL
vars.description: Redis — enables GitHub API response caching (recommended in production)

vars.name: MCP_SECRET
vars.description: If set, HTTP /mcp endpoint requires x-mcp-secret header to match

vars.name: CACHE_TTL_DEFAULT
vars.description: Default cache TTL in seconds. GitHub profiles are cached for this duration.

vars.name: PAGESPEED_API_KEY
vars.description: Google PageSpeed Insights API key — enables authenticated requests for higher rate limits when include_lighthouse is used. Works without a key at low volume.

vars.name: METRICS_INTERVAL
vars.description: Emit a metrics summary log every N tool calls

content.body: Most ATS systems filter junior candidates on CV keywords — but junior developers and career-changers often have no work history to put on a CV. Screw U, ATS bypasses the CV entirely and scores candidates on their actual GitHub activity.

Give it a GitHub username and a role, and it tells you: fit score (0–100), whether to interview, which concepts they've demonstrated, which are missing, and a plain-English summary of how their skills have grown over time.
Scoped to junior and career-changer candidates. Scoring is intentionally generous on trajectory — a candidate who is clearly improving is a stronger hire than one with a static skill set.

items.label: score_candidate
items.summary: Score a GitHub profile against a role — returns a hiring signal with fit score, recommendation, and breakdown

items.label: Role definitions
items.summary: Plain markdown files in knowledge/roles/ — easy to extend with new roles or adjust concept weights

content.body: Connect the server to your AI client (Claude Desktop, Cursor, or any MCP-compatible tool). Ask it to score a candidate by GitHub username and role. The server fetches their public GitHub activity and returns a structured hiring signal — no CV required.

content.label: Example prompt
content.body: Score GitHub user 'johndoe' for the junior-frontend role.
